meta.title: Addiction Medicine Practice Software | MAT Management & Behavioral RTM | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to addiction medicine practice operations. $7,500/month in behavioral RTM. Sublocade and Vivitrol injection scheduling. PDMP compliance tracking. Private, stigma-free check-in.

hero.subhead: Your MAT patients need monitoring between visits — that's billable RTM. Your injection patients need scheduling that doesn't miss doses. Your compliance workflows need documentation that survives audits. And your patients need a check-in process that doesn't broadcast their diagnosis to the waiting room.

sections.content:
Addiction medicine practices operate under constraints that other specialties don't face. DEA scrutiny on controlled substance prescribing. PDMP check requirements for every visit. UDS protocols that must be documented meticulously. Patient populations with high no-show rates. Stigma that affects how patients experience care from the moment they walk in.

At the same time, addiction medicine has significant untapped revenue. Medication-assisted treatment patients — whether on buprenorphine, naltrexone, or other medications — require between-visit monitoring. Cravings, triggers, mood, sleep, medication adherence. This is data that matters clinically and qualifies for Remote Therapeutic Monitoring billing.

The average addiction medicine practice with 60 MAT patients is leaving $5,000-10,000 per month on the table in unbilled RTM.

Beyond RTM, operational challenges cost money:

Injection scheduling: Sublocade is monthly. Vivitrol is monthly. Miss an injection, patient relapses, treatment restarts. But tracking injection schedules manually means patients fall through cracks.

Compliance documentation: PDMP checks, treatment agreements, UDS interpretation — every visit requires compliance steps. When the DEA audits, you need documentation that every step happened for every patient.

Patient experience: Your patients face stigma everywhere. If your waiting room, check-in process, or patient flow broadcasts 'addiction clinic,' you're adding to that stigma.

This guide covers the operational systems that capture revenue, ensure compliance, and treat patients with dignity.

Remote Therapeutic Monitoring now covers behavioral health conditions. CMS finalized behavioral health RTM codes effective January 2024. Substance use disorder qualifies.

Your MAT patients should be tracking between visits: cravings, triggers, mood, sleep quality, medication adherence, social support engagement, high-risk situations encountered. This data informs treatment — dose adjustments, therapy referrals, level of care decisions.

Most of this monitoring already happens informally. Patients call when cravings spike. They message about triggers. They report at visits. RTM systematizes and bills it.

**The CPT codes:**

98975: RTM setup — initial enrollment, patient education. ~$20-25.
98977: RTM device supply — monthly platform access. ~$50-60.
98980: RTM treatment management — first 20 minutes of clinical review. ~$50-70.
98981: Additional 20-minute increments for complex patients.

Behavioral health RTM may also use 98976 for mental health device supply where appropriate.

Typical monthly billing: $100-150 per patient depending on codes used and payer.

**What MAT patients track:**

Cravings: Frequency, intensity, triggers identified. This is core monitoring for addiction treatment.

Mood and sleep: Depression, anxiety, and sleep disruption correlate with relapse risk. Tracking identifies patients who need intervention.

Medication adherence: For oral medications (buprenorphine/naloxone), are they taking as prescribed? For injection patients, are they showing up?

Recovery engagement: AA/NA meeting attendance, therapy attendance, sponsor contact. Engagement predicts outcomes.

High-risk situations: Did the patient encounter a trigger situation? How did they cope? What support is needed?

**The math for an addiction medicine practice:**

60 MAT patients × $125/month = $7,500/month = $90,000/year.

This is revenue for clinical work that's already happening — monitoring patients, reviewing between-visit data, making treatment adjustments. clinIQ documents it and makes it billable.

**What clinIQ does for behavioral RTM:**

Addiction-specific templates: Tracking prompts designed for SUD recovery — cravings, triggers, meeting attendance, mood — not generic health questions.

Alert thresholds: Craving spike? Missed meetings? Mood decline? Alerts trigger so your team can intervene before relapse.

Automatic time tracking: Every check-in review, every message, every clinical interaction documented toward billing threshold.

Provider dashboard: Which patients are stable in recovery? Which are struggling? Which need immediate outreach?

sections.h2: Sublocade and Vivitrol Scheduling: Zero Missed Doses
sections.content:
Extended-release injectable medications transformed addiction treatment. Sublocade (buprenorphine) and Vivitrol (naltrexone) provide steady medication levels without daily dosing decisions. But they require monthly injections — miss one, and the patient is at risk.

**The injection scheduling challenge:**

Sublocade: Monthly injection, typically every 28-30 days. Requires prior authorization for many payers. If injection is missed, patient may need bridge medication.

Vivitrol: Monthly injection, every 28 days. Requires opioid-free period before initiation. If injection is missed, patient may need re-induction protocol.

Both: Patients with substance use disorder have higher no-show rates than general medical patients. Life instability, transportation issues, and ambivalence all contribute.

**What goes wrong without systematic tracking:**

Patient's injection was due last week. No one noticed. Patient hasn't called. By the time someone realizes, they've been without medication for 2 weeks and may have relapsed.

Prior auth expired before injection was scheduled. Patient shows up, insurance won't cover it, patient leaves without injection.

Patient no-showed. No one followed up. Patient is now lost to care.

**What clinIQ does for injection scheduling:**

Injection pipeline: Every injection patient visible with last injection date, next due date, and scheduling status.

Proactive scheduling: 'Injection due in 7 days, not yet scheduled' triggers outreach before the due date.

No-show follow-up: Patient missed injection appointment? Immediate alert. Same-day outreach protocol activates.

Prior auth tracking: Auth status, expiration date, and renewal needed — visible per patient. No expired auths discovered at appointment time.

**The clinical stakes:**

Missed injection → medication gap → potential relapse → treatment restart → potential overdose.

This isn't an administrative nicety. Systematic injection tracking is patient safety. clinIQ ensures no patient falls through the cracks.

Addiction medicine operates under DEA scrutiny. Buprenorphine prescribing has specific regulations. Every patient encounter requires documented compliance steps. When audits happen — and they do happen — you need documentation that survives review.

**The compliance requirements:**

PDMP check: Required by most states for every controlled substance prescription. Must be documented.

Treatment agreement: Patient signed agreement to treatment protocols, UDS, pill counts, etc. Must be current.

Urine drug screen: Regular testing per protocol. Results must be reviewed and documented. Unexpected results require documented response.

Pill counts: For oral medication patients, random pill counts per protocol.

Counseling verification: Many payers require concurrent counseling. Documentation that patient is engaged.

**What clinIQ does for compliance:**

Compliance checklist per patient: Treatment agreement (current/expired), last PDMP check, last UDS, counseling engagement — visible at every visit.

Workflow integration: Before provider encounter, system verifies: PDMP checked? UDS resulted? Treatment agreement current? Missing items surface before the visit, not during audit.

Audit trail: Every compliance event timestamped and attributed. PDMP check documented at 10:47 AM on 3/15/25 by Jane Smith, RN. UDS reviewed at 2:30 PM on 3/15/25 by Dr. Johnson.

Alert for gaps: Treatment agreement expiring in 7 days. UDS overdue per protocol. PDMP not checked in 30 days. Problems surface before they become compliance failures.

**The DEA audit scenario:**

DEA auditor asks: 'How do you ensure PDMP checks happen for every patient encounter?'

Old answer: 'It's our policy. Staff are trained to do it.'

New answer: 'The system requires PDMP documentation before provider encounter. Here's the audit trail showing every check for the past 18 months, timestamped and attributed.'

One answer survives scrutiny. One doesn't.

Your patients face stigma every day. They don't need more of it when they're trying to get well.

Traditional waiting room check-in broadcasts diagnosis. The receptionist asks questions across a counter. Other patients hear. The clipboard has 'addiction medicine' in the header. The waiting room mixes patients from all visit types.

Patients notice. Some don't come back.

**The stigma-free check-in challenge:**

How do you confirm identity, collect copays, and verify information without announcing to everyone that this person is here for addiction treatment?

**What clinIQ does for private check-in:**

Kiosk check-in: Patient checks in on a tablet or kiosk. No verbal exchange at a reception desk. No clipboard visible to other patients.

Phone-based check-in: Patient can check in from their phone before arriving. They walk in already checked in — straight to a seat or straight to a room.

Private waiting: If your space allows, patients can wait in their cars and receive a text when the provider is ready. No waiting room time at all.

Minimal visible identifiers: Check-in screens don't display 'ADDICTION MEDICINE' in large letters. The experience is medical care, not stigma.

**The patient experience difference:**

Patient walks in. Taps their name on a kiosk. Sits down. Gets called back. No one knows why they're there.

Compare to: Patient walks in. Receptionist says loudly, 'Here for your Suboxone appointment?' Other patients look. Patient cringes.

Dignity matters. Patients who feel respected come back. Retention is treatment.

Addiction medicine visits have specific stages that differ from general medical visits.

Typical MAT visit: Check-in → UDS collection → Vitals → With MA (med reconciliation, compliance review) → With Provider → Prescription handling → Checkout.

Injection visit: Check-in → Vitals → With MA → Injection administration → Post-injection observation (Sublocade requires 30+ minute observation) → Checkout.

Induction visit: Extended visit with education, consent, observation, and dosing.

**What clinIQ does for patient flow:**

Stage-based tracking: Every patient's current stage visible. Check-in, UDS, vitals, MA, provider, observation, checkout — all visible.

UDS status visibility: UDS collected? Resulted? Reviewed? This is critical information that can't be missed.

Observation timer: For Sublocade injections, post-injection observation time is tracked. 'Injection at 10:15, observation until 10:45.' No early discharges.

Queue by role: MAs see their queue. Providers see theirs. Front desk sees everyone. Each role knows what to do next.

Wait time management: Patients in recovery may have less tolerance for waiting (triggers, anxiety, life obligations). Minimizing wait time is clinically important.

**The high-volume challenge:**

MAT practices often run high volume — 40-60 patients per day. Efficient flow is essential. Without visibility, the practice bogs down, waits extend, and patients leave.

clinIQ maintains visibility at scale. You see the whole practice, not just the patient in front of you.

Addiction medicine practices have specific workflows and compliance requirements. Implementation accounts for both.

**Week 1: Discovery and Configuration**

We map your workflows: MAT visits, injection visits, induction protocols, group sessions (if applicable). Compliance checkpoints configured.

RTM templates configured for addiction recovery: craving tracking, meeting attendance, mood monitoring.

Check-in configured for privacy: kiosk, phone-based, or combined approach.

**Week 2: Training**

Front desk learns private check-in. MAs learn flow tracking and UDS documentation. Providers learn RTM review and compliance verification. All staff understand the privacy-first approach.

**Week 3: Go-Live**

Live operation with support. RTM enrollment begins. Compliance tracking activates. Injection scheduling systematized.

**Week 4 and Beyond**

RTM billing starts. Injection tracking prevents missed doses. Compliance documentation audit-ready.

**ROI Timeline:**

Month 1: RTM revenue begins with initial enrollments. Injection tracking immediate.

Month 3: RTM at scale (40-60 patients). No-show follow-up reducing missed injections. Compliance documentation complete.

Month 6: Steady state. $6,000-10,000/month in RTM revenue. Zero missed injections from scheduling gaps. Audit-ready compliance trail.

**Investment:**

Professional: $499/month — Full suite including RTM, injection tracking, compliance documentation, patient flow

Implementation: $750 one-time — Workflow mapping, template configuration, training

EHR Integration: Custom quote based on EHR and scope

faqs.q: Is behavioral health RTM now billable?
faqs.a: Yes. CMS finalized behavioral health RTM codes effective 2024. Substance use disorder qualifies. CPT 98980/98981 for treatment management, with applicable device supply codes. Most commercial payers are following Medicare's lead.

faqs.q: How do you track injection schedules?
faqs.a: Every injection patient visible with last injection date, next due date, and scheduling status. Proactive alerts when injections are coming due. Immediate follow-up protocol when patients no-show.

faqs.q: What compliance steps do you track?
faqs.a: PDMP checks, treatment agreements, UDS collection and review, pill counts, counseling verification. Each documented with timestamp and attribution. Audit-ready trail.
Kiosk or phone-based check-in. No verbal exchange at reception. No visible 'addiction medicine' branding. Patients can wait in cars and receive text when ready. Dignity preserved.
If you run group therapy or education sessions, those can be scheduled and tracked separately from individual visits. Attendance documented per patient.

faqs.q: Does this work with X-waiver/DATA 2000 requirements?
faqs.a: We support documentation requirements for buprenorphine prescribing under current federal regulations. The system adapts as regulations evolve.

faqs.q: What if patients don't have smartphones for RTM?
faqs.a: Most patients do — smartphone ownership is high even in challenging populations. For those who don't, family/caregiver proxy enrollment or simplified tracking via text message is available.

faqs.q: How do you handle patients who relapse?
faqs.a: RTM tracking can identify early warning signs — increased cravings, missed meetings, mood changes. Alerts trigger intervention before full relapse. If relapse occurs, patient status and treatment protocol can be adjusted in the system.

testimonial.quote: We lost patients because injections got missed. Nobody was tracking due dates systematically. Now every injection patient is visible. Zero missed doses in six months.
